did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a market-approved deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN), Moleanalyzer-pro, to perform binary classification of dermoscopic images for melanoma recognition.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'Original Research' in the header. The study describes a cross-sectional analysis involving original experimental methods, specifically applying a deep learning convolutional neural network to dermoscopic image sets to assess diagnostic performance, and reports new results including sensitivity, specificity, and AUC metrics.

title: Association between different scale bars in dermoscopic images and diagnostic performance of a market-approved deep learning convolutional neural network for melanoma recognition
brief_summary: This study investigated whether scale bars in dermoscopic images affect the diagnostic accuracy of a market-approved deep learning convolutional neural network (Moleanalyzer-pro) for melanoma recognition. The researchers applied the CNN to seven dermoscopic image sets depicting 130 melanocytic lesions, including images with digitally superimposed scale bars from different manufacturers and a control set without scale bars. The main evaluation approach involved assessing sensitivity, specificity, and the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) for the CNN's binary classification of melanoma versus nevus. The results showed that while scale bars did not significantly alter sensitivity, four of the tested scale bars induced a significant decrease in specificity. The study concludes that the presence and type of scale bars can negatively impact the diagnostic performance of this specific AI model.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task; 23 melanocytic lesions were melanomas out of 130 total.
One of the two classes in the binary classification task; 107 nevi were included out of 130 total melanocytic lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into melanoma or nevus categories.
all_task_types.details: The study assessed the CNN's binary classification of images with or without superimposed scale bars for melanoma recognition. The model predicted discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. nevus) for 130 melanocytic lesions. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and ROC AUC.

datasets_used.name: Seven dermoscopic image sets (Control and Scale Bar variants)
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images depicting the same 130 melanocytic lesions. The dataset consists of a control set without scale bars and six sets with digitally superimposed scale bars from different manufacturers. The images were submitted to the Moleanalyzer-pro CNN for analysis. Total images submitted were 910.

use.testing: Used as the input dataset for the Moleanalyzer-pro CNN to assess diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) in a binary classification task.
use.other: The CNN itself was trained on a separate dataset of more than 150,000 images, but this specific collection of 130 lesions was used for the study's evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty for sensitivity, specificity, and ROC AUC metrics.
To assess statistical significance of changes in sensitivity and specificity induced by different scale bars compared to the control.
To calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for evaluating the diagnostic performance of the CNN.

hypothesis_testing.test: Not explicitly named (likely binomial test or similar for proportions given the context of sensitivity/specificity with confidence intervals), but p-values are reported.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The presence of scale bars does not significantly alter the diagnostic performance (sensitivity or specificity) of the CNN compared to images without scale bars.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The presence of scale bars significantly alters the diagnostic performance (sensitivity or specificity) of the CNN compared to images without scale bars.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Scale bars were not associated with significant changes in sensitivity (all p > 1.0, likely a typo for p > 0.05 or p=1.0 indicating no difference). However, four scale bars induced a significant decrease in the CNN’s specificity (all p < 0.001).

Control Set (No Scale Bars).notes: This set served as the baseline for comparison. It consisted of images of 130 melanocytic lesions (107 nevi, 23 melanomas) without digitally superimposed scale bars.

Scale Bar Sets (Six different manufactur.notes: Scale bars were not associated with significant changes in sensitivity (all p > 1.0). However, four of the six scale bars induced a significant decrease in the CNN’s specificity (all p < 0.001).

architecture_details.manufacturer: FotoFinder Systems Inc., Bad Birnbach, Germany

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification of melanoma vs. nevus

preprocessing_techniques: Digitally superimposed scale bars from different manufacturers onto dermoscopic images
Creation of a control set without scale bars
conclusions: The presence of digitally superimposed scale bars in dermoscopic images does not significantly alter the sensitivity of the market-approved deep learning convolutional neural network (Moleanalyzer-pro) for melanoma recognition.
Four out of six tested scale bars induced a significant decrease in the specificity of the CNN, with specificity dropping to a range of 0% to 43.9%.

The type and presence of scale bars can negatively impact the diagnostic performance of this specific AI model, highlighting the need to investigate causes for false diagnoses before broader application of deep learning CNNs.
